steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome to the Multi-Agent Multi-Repo Roundup lab. This is an enterprise-level exercise where you will experience, and solve, the cross-repo context gap using GitHub Copilot.

Your workspace has been set up with two repositories: a CCPR template (your organization's baseline Copilot configuration) and the OctoCAT Supply frontend (a React application). The frontend calls an API that lives in a separate repository, but that repo is not in your workspace yet. You will feel the context gap firsthand, then use Copilot to bridge it.

Along the way, you will layer custom instructions across repos, configure MCP servers for cross-repo access, design orchestration workflows, and build governance models for multi-repo automation.

steps.title: Explore Your Workspace
steps.instruction: Open the Explorer and review the two repos already in your workspace: the CCPR template (ccpr_root) and the OctoCAT Supply frontend (octocat-supply-frontend). Browse the frontend's src/api/config.ts; notice how it connects to an API backend via a configurable URL.

steps.title: Experience the Context Gap
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat in Agent mode. Ask Copilot about the API the frontend depends on:

What endpoints does the OctoCAT Supply API expose? What data models does it use?

Copilot cannot answer accurately because the API code is not in your workspace. This is the cross-repo context gap in action. The frontend references routes and models that live in a completely separate repository.

steps.title: Bridge the Gap with Copilot
steps.instruction:
Now use Copilot to bring the missing context into your workspace. In agent mode, paste:

Clone https://github.com/ekuris-repos/octocat-supply-api and add it to this workspace

Copilot will clone the API repository and add it to your multi-root workspace. Once it finishes, you should see three folders in the Explorer: ccpr_root, octocat-supply-frontend, and octocat-supply-api.
steps.tip: This demonstrates a real-world pattern: using Copilot as a workspace orchestrator. Instead of manual git commands, the agent handles cloning and workspace configuration for you.

steps.title: Verify Cross-Repo Context
steps.instruction:
With all three repos in your workspace, ask the same question again:

What endpoints does the OctoCAT Supply API expose? What data models does it use?

Now Copilot can read the API source code directly. Compare the accuracy of this response to the earlier attempt. The workspace boundary defines what Copilot knows.

steps.title: Examine the CCPR Template
steps.instruction:
Open the Explorer and browse the ccpr_root folder. This is your organization's Central Code Prompt Repository (CCPR), a template that standardizes Copilot's behavior across all repos. Notice the structure:

• .github/copilot-instructions.md: org-level baseline
• .github/instructions/: path-scoped instruction files
• .github/agents/: reusable agent definitions
• .github/prompts/: shared prompt files
• .github/skills/: domain-specific skills

Compare this to the .github/ folders in the API and frontend repos. The CCPR template is the source; the individual repos inherit from it.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Examine Org-Level Instructions
steps.instruction:
Open the ccpr_root/.github/copilot-instructions.md file. This is your organization's baseline Copilot configuration; it defines standards that apply across every repo that inherits from the CCPR template.

Notice how it sets rules for error handling, type safety, naming conventions, and review priorities. These instructions are automatically loaded by Copilot for every developer working in a repo derived from this template.
steps.tip: The CCPR template is the single source of truth for org-level instructions. When you update it, every repo that forked from it can pull the changes.

steps.title: Compare Repo-Level Instructions
steps.instruction:
Now open octocat-supply-api/.github/copilot-instructions.md and compare it to the CCPR template version. The API repo has project-specific instructions that layer on top of the org baseline, covering architecture details, tech stack, and review conventions specific to this Express + SQLite API.

Then open octocat-supply-frontend/.github/copilot-instructions.md and compare. Each repo customizes the baseline for its own needs. This is the layered approach in action.

steps.title: Verify Layered Instructions
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat in agent mode. Ask:

What instructions have you loaded for this workspace? List the rules from each instructions file you can see across all workspace folders.

Copilot should reference instruction files from all three repos. Then test the layering:

Generate a new API endpoint for the OctoCAT Supply API that lists product categories. Follow the patterns and conventions from the instruction files.

Verify the output follows both the org-level conventions and the API repo's specific patterns (repository pattern, Swagger docs, custom error types).

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Explore Existing Prompt Files
steps.instruction:
The API repo already has prompt files from the CCPR template. Open the Explorer and browse octocat-supply-api/.github/prompts/. You will find prompts for DRY violations, handoff to the coding agent, and general handoff patterns.

Open handoff-to-copilot-coding-agent.prompt.md and read its structure. It uses YAML frontmatter for metadata and Markdown for the prompt body.

steps.title: Create a Cross-Repo Prompt File
steps.instruction:
Create a new prompt file that leverages cross-repo context. In agent mode, ask:

Create a new prompt file at octocat-supply-frontend/.github/prompts/sync-api-types.prompt.md. It should generate TypeScript interfaces in the frontend that match the API's data models. The prompt should instruct Copilot to read the API's model files from the workspace, extract the type definitions, and generate matching frontend types in src/api/types.ts.

This is a cross-repo prompt; it requires context from the API repo to generate code in the frontend repo. It only works because both repos are in the same workspace.

steps.title: Test the Cross-Repo Prompt
steps.instruction:
Type / in Copilot Chat to open the prompt picker. Select sync-api-types and run it.

Review the generated types. Do they match the models in the API's src/models/ directory? This demonstrates how prompt files can encode cross-repo knowledge. The template encapsulates the 'how to sync types' pattern so any developer can run it without knowing the API repo's internals.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Explorer: Ctrl+Shift+E
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Examine Existing MCP Configuration
steps.instruction:
The frontend repo already has an MCP configuration. Open octocat-supply-frontend/.vscode/mcp.json and examine its contents. You will see a Playwright MCP server configured for browser automation testing.

MCP servers extend Copilot's capabilities beyond code context. Each server provides specialized tools that Copilot can invoke during agent mode conversations.
steps.tip: MCP (Model Context Protocol) servers give Copilot access to external systems, APIs, and tools. They are the primary mechanism for extending Copilot's reach beyond the workspace.

steps.title: Add the GitHub MCP Server
steps.instruction:
The GitHub MCP Server gives Copilot the ability to search, read, and interact with repositories outside your workspace, including repos not in this workspace. In agent mode, ask Copilot to add it:

Add a GitHub remote MCP server to my workspace mcp.json. Use the hosted version at https://api.githubcopilot.com/mcp/ with toolsets for repos, issues, pull_requests, and code_security.

After Copilot adds the configuration, verify it appears in .vscode/mcp.json.

steps.title: Test Cross-Repo MCP Access
steps.instruction:
With the GitHub MCP Server configured, test its cross-repo capabilities. In agent mode, ask:

Using the GitHub MCP server, search for repositories in the ekuris-repos organization. List what you find and describe each repo's purpose.

The GitHub MCP Server provides live, authenticated access to your organization's repositories without adding them to the workspace. This is how you access repos that are too large or sensitive to clone locally.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Design a Custom Cross-Repo MCP Tool
steps.instruction:
Use agent mode to scaffold a custom MCP server that bridges the API and frontend repos. Ask:

Create an MCP server in TypeScript using @modelcontextprotocol/sdk that exposes a tool called "get-api-spec". The tool should read the api-swagger.json file from the octocat-supply-api workspace folder and return the endpoints, request/response schemas, and status codes as structured text. This gives any repo in the workspace programmatic access to the API contract.

Review the generated code. Verify it uses the MCP SDK's standard tool registration pattern and does not hardcode file paths.

steps.title: Add a Second Cross-Repo Tool
steps.instruction:
Extend the MCP server with a second tool. In agent mode, ask:

Add another tool called "get-api-models" that reads all TypeScript files from the octocat-supply-api/src/models/ directory, extracts the exported interfaces and types, and returns them as a formatted list. This helps the frontend team generate matching types without browsing the API source code.

Having multiple tools on one MCP server creates a composable cross-repo context layer. Each tool answers a different question about the sibling repository.

steps.title: Register the Custom Server
steps.instruction:
Add your custom MCP server to the workspace MCP configuration. In your .vscode/mcp.json, add the server alongside Playwright and GitHub:

{
"cross-repo-specs": {
"command": "npx",
"args": ["tsx", "cross-repo-spec-server.ts"]
}
}

Now Copilot can access the GitHub MCP Server for remote repos, Playwright for browser testing, and your custom server for local cross-repo data, all simultaneously.

steps.title: Check MCP Server Output
steps.instruction:
Open the Output panel and select GitHub Copilot from the dropdown. Look for log entries confirming that all configured MCP servers have been detected and initialized.

If a server fails to start, the Output panel shows the error. Common issues include missing dependencies or incorrect command paths.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Output Panel: Ctrl+Shift+U

steps.title: Write a Cross-Repo Issue Template
steps.instruction:
The OctoCAT Supply repos need coordinated changes when the API contract changes. Create a cross-repo issue template in the API repo. In agent mode, ask:

Create a file at octocat-supply-api/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/cross-repo-update.md. It should be a GitHub issue template for propagating API changes to the frontend. Include fields for: the upstream PR/commit that changed the API, a summary of what changed, a checklist of required frontend changes (update types, update API calls, update tests), and a context section for pasting relevant API diffs or OpenAPI spec changes.

This template embeds enough cross-repo context for the coding agent to work independently.
steps.tip: Embed as much cross-repo context as possible in the issue body. The coding agent reads the full issue description when planning its work.

steps.title: Simulate a Cross-Repo Issue
steps.instruction:
Imagine the API team adds a new status field to the Supplier model. In agent mode, design coordinated issues:

The OctoCAT Supply API just added a "status" field (enum: active, inactive, suspended) to the Supplier model. Write two GitHub issue bodies: one for the API repo (add the field to the database migration and seed data) and one for the frontend repo (update the Supplier display components and add filtering by status). Each issue should contain enough context — diffs, file references, and before/after examples — for the coding agent to work without asking questions.

Review the generated issues. Each should be self-contained with all the cross-repo context embedded.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Create a Cross-Repo Dispatch Workflow
steps.instruction:
Create a GitHub Actions workflow in the API repo that notifies the frontend repo when the API releases a new version. In agent mode, ask:

Create a workflow at octocat-supply-api/.github/workflows/notify-frontend.yml. It should trigger on release published events and dispatch a repository_dispatch event to ekuris-repos/octocat-supply-frontend with event-type "api-update". The payload should include the release version, the source repo name, and a link to the release.

This is the coordinator pattern: the upstream repo explicitly signals its consumers.

steps.title: Create the Receiver Workflow
steps.instruction:
Now create the matching workflow in the frontend repo. In agent mode, ask:

Create a workflow at octocat-supply-frontend/.github/workflows/handle-api-update.yml. It should trigger on repository_dispatch with type "api-update". The workflow should create a GitHub issue in the frontend repo with the title "Update for API ${version}", including the release details from the payload, a checklist of likely frontend changes (update types, test API responses, update docs), and labels "cross-repo" and "agent-eligible".

This completes the dispatch chain: API release triggers frontend issue creation.
steps.tip: The receiver workflow creates an agent-eligible issue. If your org has the coding agent enabled, you can assign it automatically using the GitHub API.

steps.title: Add Agent Auto-Assignment
steps.instruction:
Extend the receiver workflow to automatically assign the coding agent. In agent mode, ask:

Modify handle-api-update.yml to also assign the "copilot" user as an assignee on the newly created issue, so the coding agent picks it up automatically. Add error handling in case the agent is not available for this repo.

Review the result. This completes the automation chain: API release → dispatch → issue creation → agent assignment.

steps.title: Validate Workflow Syntax
steps.instruction:
Open a Terminal and validate your workflow files:

npx yaml-lint octocat-supply-api/.github/workflows/notify-frontend.yml octocat-supply-frontend/.github/workflows/handle-api-update.yml

In production, use actionlint for full GitHub Actions validation. Catching syntax errors early prevents failed dispatches across your repo network.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Terminal: Ctrl+`

steps.title: Design a Consumer Registry
steps.instruction:
As your organization grows, the API repo may serve multiple frontends and services. Create a consumer registry that drives fan-out automation. In agent mode, ask:

Create a file called consumer-registry.json in the octocat-supply-api repo. It should list the frontend repo (ekuris-repos/octocat-supply-frontend) as a high-priority consumer, plus two fictional consumers (a mobile-app repo at medium priority and an analytics-dashboard at low priority). Include rollout configuration: priority-ordered strategy, batch size of 1, and wait-for-merge between batches.

steps.title: Generate the Fan-Out Workflow
steps.instruction:
Use agent mode to create a workflow that reads the registry and fans out in priority order:

Create a GitHub Actions workflow at octocat-supply-api/.github/workflows/fan-out-update.yml. It should read consumer-registry.json, group consumers by priority, and dispatch repository_dispatch events in batches. High-priority repos go first (the real frontend repo). The workflow should include a tracking step that creates a master issue listing all downstream repos and their update status.

Review the generated workflow. This is the coordinator pattern: automated, sequenced, and tracked across real repositories.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Audit Agent Permissions
steps.instruction:
Open Copilot Chat and ask it to help you audit your cross-repo automation's permission model. Reference the real repos in your workspace:

I'm setting up cross-repo automation between octocat-supply-api and octocat-supply-frontend using GitHub Actions and the coding agent. Help me design a least-privilege permission model. I need: (1) minimum GitHub App permissions for creating issues and dispatching events between these repos, (2) MCP server token scopes for read-only access to both repos, and (3) a checklist of branch protection rules for agent-created PRs.

steps.title: Create a CODEOWNERS File
steps.instruction:
Create a CODEOWNERS file in the API repo that ensures agent-generated changes get proper review. In agent mode, ask:

Create a CODEOWNERS file at octocat-supply-api/.github/CODEOWNERS. Default owners should be the platform team. Database migrations should require DBA review. GitHub Actions workflows should require DevOps review. The Swagger spec (api-swagger.json) should require API governance review.

CODEOWNERS applies to all PRs, including those created by the coding agent. This ensures human review for sensitive changes.

steps.title: Review Changes in Source Control
steps.instruction:
Open the Source Control panel. In a multi-root workspace, you see each repo's changes listed separately under its folder name. Review the files you have created or modified across all three workspace roots.

This is how teams audit agent-generated changes before committing. Each root is an independent Git repository with its own staging area and commit history.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the panels for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I
• Source Control: Ctrl+Shift+G

steps.title: Build Your Enterprise Action Plan
steps.instruction:
Use agent mode to generate a prioritized rollout plan for your organization. Reference the real artifacts you built:

Based on the CCPR template, the OctoCAT Supply API and frontend repos, the custom instructions, MCP server configs, GitHub Actions workflows, issue templates, and CODEOWNERS files in this workspace — generate a 30-60-90 day action plan for rolling out multi-repo Copilot automation across an enterprise with 200+ repositories. Prioritize by effort-to-impact ratio. Include milestones, success metrics, and risks for each phase.

Review the plan. It should reference the specific artifacts you created during this lab.

steps.title: Identify Departing Patterns
steps.instruction:
Review the artifacts you built with a critical eye. In agent mode, ask:

Which of the patterns and configurations I built in this workspace — the CCPR template, custom MCP servers, dispatch workflows, issue templates — are most likely to be simplified or replaced by upcoming GitHub Copilot features? Flag any workarounds that exist only because native cross-repo capabilities are not yet available. For each, suggest what to watch for and when to reconsider the approach.

This exercise builds the habit of evaluating your automation for long-term maintainability as the platform evolves.

steps.title: Panels Closed
steps.instruction:
We've closed the sidebar for you.

For future reference:
• Copilot Chat: Ctrl+Shift+I

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Roundup complete! You have built a full cross-repo automation toolkit using real application repositories.

What you accomplished:
• Experienced the cross-repo context gap firsthand with the OctoCAT Supply frontend and API
• Used Copilot to clone and add the API repo, bridging the gap dynamically
• Explored layered custom instructions from the CCPR template through to repo-specific configs
• Built cross-repo prompt files that generate frontend types from API models
• Configured MCP servers (Playwright, GitHub, custom cross-repo tools) for extended Copilot capabilities
• Designed cross-repo issue templates and dispatch workflows between API and frontend repos
• Established governance with CODEOWNERS and least-privilege permission models
• Built an enterprise rollout plan grounded in real polyrepo patterns

Next courses: Agent HQ: Reporting for Duty for centralized agent governance, or SDK: Have It Your Way for building custom Copilot integrations.
